rows.answer:
**Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory**  
This classic paper found that jobs high in skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback produce higher internal motivation and job satisfaction. It supports the idea that “interesting” work matters a lot, especially when it also gives you real ownership and learning.

**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature**  
This meta-analysis showed that motivational job characteristics such as autonomy, task significance, and variety are strongly related to job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, performance, and lower burnout. The results support prioritizing interesting work, but they also show that social and contextual features matter too, so “almost exclusively” is too strong.

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation**  
This paper found that some forms of external rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, especially when rewards feel controlling. That suggests higher pay is not a clean substitute for interesting work, though pay does not always crowd out interest.

**Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis**  
This meta-analysis found that intrinsic motivation is more strongly associated with performance quality, while extrinsic incentives are more strongly associated with performance quantity. Its main implication is that interesting work is very important, but the best outcomes usually come from combining intrinsic interest with solid external conditions rather than optimizing only one.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature**  
This paper found a positive but modest relationship between pay and job satisfaction. In practice, that means salary matters, but usually less than people expect when compared with richer aspects of the work itself.

**Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis**  
This meta-analysis found that meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, work engagement, organizational commitment, and overall well-being. It supports your friends’ claim that meaningful/interesting work is often undervalued, but it does not imply that other factors like advancement, autonomy, and compensation should be ignored.

rows.answer:
- **Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory**  
  This paper found that jobs high in skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback produce higher internal motivation and job satisfaction. It is one of the strongest classic empirical foundations for the claim that “interesting” work characteristics matter a lot.

- **Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being**  
  This review argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are basic psychological needs, and work that supports them tends to improve motivation, performance, and well-being. It also summarizes evidence that external rewards can sometimes crowd out intrinsic motivation, suggesting that a salary bump may not fully offset less engaging work.

- **The Psychological Conditions of Personal Engagement and Disengagement at Work**  
  Kahn argues that meaningfulness is one of the main conditions for full engagement at work, alongside psychological safety and availability. The paper is influential because it treats meaningful, interesting work as central to whether people invest energy and identity in their jobs.

- **High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being**  
  This study found that higher income is associated with better life evaluation, but day-to-day emotional well-being rose only up to about $75,000 in the data and period studied. It supports the idea that money matters, but not necessarily as much for everyday experience as people often assume.

- **Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year**  
  This later large-scale study challenged the earlier plateau result and found that experienced well-being continued to rise with income across the observed range. Its main implication is that salary still matters, but it does not show that money outweighs work interest or meaning.

- **Money and Happiness: Rank of Income, Not Income, Affects Life Satisfaction**  
  This paper found that relative income rank predicts life satisfaction better than absolute income in the dataset studied. For job choices, it suggests that a modest salary increase may have limited impact unless it meaningfully changes your perceived standing.

- **When and Why Incentives (Don’t) Work to Modify Behavior**  
  This review concludes that incentives work best for simple tasks and are less reliable for complex, interesting, or mission-driven work, partly because they can undermine intrinsic motivation. For research roles, it strengthens the case that the content of the work itself can be more important than incremental pay.

- **Calling and Vocation at Work: Definitions and Prospects for Research and Practice**  
  This review finds that experiencing work as meaningful or “a calling” is associated with higher satisfaction and purpose. It also warns that people who highly value meaningful work can overwork or accept worse conditions, so the evidence does not support focusing on interest almost exclusively.

rows.answer:
- **Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory**  
  This paper showed that core job characteristics like skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback strongly predict internal motivation and job satisfaction. It supports the idea that interesting work has major effects on day-to-day experience, but not that it is the only variable that matters.

- **Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature**  
  This meta-analysis found that autonomy, task variety, task significance, and feedback are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction, engagement, commitment, and lower strain. It is some of the best evidence that “interesting” and well-designed work is often undervalued relative to compensation alone.

- **The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature**  
  This meta-analysis found that pay is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is modest. In practice, it suggests that once compensation is already good, differences in the nature of the work often matter more for satisfaction than additional salary.

- **A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation**  
  This review found that expected, contingent external rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for tasks people already find interesting. It supports caution about choosing mainly for money when the alternative job is substantially more engaging in itself.

- **Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis**  
  This meta-analysis found that intrinsic motivation is more strongly associated with performance quality and persistence, while extrinsic incentives are more associated with performance quantity. For creative and research-heavy work, that pattern usually favors choosing work you genuinely find interesting.

- **Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis**  
  This meta-analysis found that meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, commitment, and overall well-being, and negatively associated with turnover intentions. It supports giving substantial weight to interesting or meaningful work, but not treating it as the sole consideration.

- **Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work**  
  This paper found that people who relate to their work as a calling report higher satisfaction and meaning even when objective job conditions are similar. It is relevant because a move into management can increase well-being if leading people feels like a calling, but decrease it if it pulls you away from the work you find intrinsically absorbing.

- **Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation**  
  This review argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central psychological needs, and jobs that satisfy them produce more durable motivation and well-being than jobs driven mainly by external rewards. It implies that “interesting work” matters most when it also brings freedom, mastery, and strong social connection.

rows.answer:
- **Motivation through the design of work: Test of a theory**  
  Hackman and Oldham found that jobs high in skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback produce higher internal motivation and job satisfaction. This is one of the foundational results showing that how interesting and enriching the work itself is has major effects on how people feel about their jobs.

- **Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature**  
  This meta-analysis found that work characteristics such as autonomy, task variety, task significance, and feedback are strongly related to job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, and performance. The evidence suggests that “interesting work” is not underrated in the research literature; it is a central predictor of good work outcomes.

- **Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis**  
  Cerasoli, Nicklin, and Ford found that intrinsic motivation is a stronger predictor of performance quality than extrinsic incentives, while incentives matter more for output quantity. That supports your friends’ view in part, but it does not support focusing on interestingness almost exclusively in every case.

- **Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis**  
  Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, and Tay found that meaningful work is robustly associated with job satisfaction, work engagement, organizational commitment, and overall well-being. The effect sizes are large enough to suggest that interest and meaning are major drivers of a good career, not merely nice-to-haves.

- **Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work**  
  Wrzesniewski and colleagues showed that people who relate to their work as a calling report substantially higher satisfaction than those who treat it mainly as a job or a career ladder. The study is correlational, but it strongly supports the idea that compelling work can matter more than pure compensation.

- **Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation**  
  Gagné and Deci review evidence that autonomous motivation—doing work because it is interesting or personally endorsed—predicts persistence, performance, and well-being better than controlled motivation. The paper argues that pay and promotions matter most when they support autonomy rather than substitute for interest.

- **High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being**  
  Kahneman and Deaton found that higher income has a clearer effect on how people evaluate their lives than on day-to-day emotional well-being. If one job is only modestly higher paying, the literature suggests the daily experiential quality of the work may matter more for lived happiness than the salary bump alone.

rows.answer:
- *Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being.* This paper argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are basic psychological needs, and work that satisfies them reliably improves motivation and well-being. “Interesting” work often scores well on these dimensions, but leadership roles can too if they increase autonomy, mastery, and connection.

- *A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation.* Across experiments, expected tangible rewards tended to reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, while informational feedback could enhance it. The usual takeaway is that more money does not cleanly substitute for doing work you find inherently engaging.

- *Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory.* This paper shows that skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback predict internal work motivation and job satisfaction. It supports the view that what the work is like matters a great deal, not just what it pays.

- *Work Design Characteristics and Outcomes: A Meta-Analysis and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature.* This meta-analysis finds that motivating work characteristics—especially autonomy, task significance, knowledge characteristics, and social support—are strongly associated with satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower strain. It suggests interesting work is not underrated in the literature, but it is only one part of a broader work-design package.

- *The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature.* This meta-analysis finds that pay level is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is modest. Higher salary helps, yet it is usually not among the strongest drivers of whether people actually like their jobs.

- *Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person-Job, Person-Organization, Person-Group, and Person-Supervisor Fit.* This paper shows that person-job and person-organization fit are robustly related to job satisfaction, commitment, performance, and turnover intentions. If leading a small team matches a strong personal aspiration, that fit can matter a lot even if the subject matter is less interesting.

- *Some Key Differences Between a Happy Life and a Meaningful Life.* This paper finds that meaning and happiness overlap but are not the same: meaning is more tied to contribution, identity, and investing in valued goals, while happiness is more tied to immediate enjoyment and need satisfaction. It implies that interesting work is valuable, but roles that feel important or identity-relevant can also be worth a lot even when they are less enjoyable moment to moment.

- *High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being.* This paper finds that income is more strongly linked to how people evaluate their lives than to their day-to-day emotional experience. Extra pay may therefore matter more for life assessment than for how good work actually feels on ordinary days.

- *Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year.* Using large-scale real-time reports, this paper finds experienced well-being continues to rise with income beyond earlier proposed plateaus. It is a useful reminder that money still matters, even if it does not appear to dominate intrinsic job quality in most work-motivation research.

rows.answer:
- **Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory**  
  This classic paper found that jobs high in variety, significance, autonomy, and feedback produce higher internal motivation and job satisfaction. It is one of the strongest foundations for the view that the nature of the work itself matters a lot, not just compensation.

- **The Nature of Work Design: A Synthesis of the Literature and Directions for Future Research**  
  This review concludes that autonomy, task variety, significance, and knowledge demands are robust predictors of motivation, satisfaction, performance, and well-being. It supports the idea that “interesting work” is very important, while also showing it is only one part of a broader job-design picture.

- **The “What” and “Why” of Goal Pursuits: Human Needs and the Self-Determination of Behavior**  
  This paper argues that intrinsically motivated activities and environments supporting autonomy and competence lead to better motivation and well-being than extrinsically driven ones. In career terms, work you find genuinely engaging is usually more sustainable than work chosen mainly for higher pay.

- **Some Key Differences between a Happy Life and a Meaningful Life**  
  This paper distinguishes meaning from immediate pleasure and shows that effortful, demanding activities can contribute strongly to a meaningful life. That is relevant because “interesting” work may be more fulfilling over time even when it is not the easiest or highest-paid option.

- **Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person-Job, Person-Organization, Person-Group, and Person-Supervisor Fit**  
  This meta-analysis found that better fit predicts higher job satisfaction, stronger commitment, better performance, and lower turnover intentions. It suggests the best choice is often the role that fits both your interests and your aspirations, not the one that wins on any single dimension.

- **High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being**  
  This paper found that higher income was strongly linked to overall life evaluation, but much less to day-to-day emotional well-being. It is often cited as evidence that modest salary gains may matter less to lived experience than the quality of the work itself.

- **Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year**  
  This paper challenged the earlier plateau finding and reported that experienced well-being continues to rise with income on average. The takeaway is that money is not irrelevant, but it does not erase the importance of intrinsic job qualities like interest and autonomy.

rows.answer:
**Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory**  
Hackman and Oldham found that jobs high in skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback produce greater internal motivation and job satisfaction. In plain terms, work that feels interesting and meaningful tends to matter a lot for how good the job feels.

**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature**  
Humphrey, Nahrgang, and Morgeson’s meta-analysis showed that motivational job characteristics such as autonomy, task variety, and task significance are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction, engagement, and lower turnover intentions. The evidence supports your friends’ core claim that interesting work is often underweighted in career decisions.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature**  
Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, and Rich found that pay level has only a weak-to-moderate relationship with job satisfaction. Higher salary helps, but it is usually much less powerful than people expect relative to the nature of the work itself.

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation**  
Deci, Koestner, and Ryan found that some extrinsic rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation, especially for tasks that are already interesting. This does not mean money is bad, but it does suggest that more pay is not a reliable substitute for genuinely engaging work.

**Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation**  
Gagné and Deci reviewed evidence showing that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central drivers of high-quality motivation and well-being at work. This implies that “interestingness” is crucial, but not sufficient by itself if the role also changes your autonomy, growth, or relationships.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit**  
Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, and Johnson found that person–job and person–organization fit strongly predict job satisfaction, commitment, and intentions to stay. The broader lesson is that research does not support focusing almost exclusively on interesting tasks; fit with the role, culture, and people matters a great deal too.

**Fostering Meaningful Work in Organizations: A Multi-level Review and Integration**  
Lysova, Allan, Dik, Duffy, and Steger reviewed evidence that meaningful work is consistently associated with higher well-being, engagement, and performance. Their review supports the view that meaningful or interesting work is often undervalued, while also emphasizing that it emerges from multiple factors, not just task content alone.

rows.answer:
**Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory**  
This classic study found that jobs high in skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback produce higher internal motivation and job satisfaction. It is foundational evidence that the day-to-day interestingness and structure of work strongly affect how good a job feels.

**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature**  
Across 259 studies, autonomy, task variety, task significance, and feedback showed robust links to satisfaction, internal motivation, performance, and lower strain. The same meta-analysis also found social support and context matter independently, so “interesting work” is important but not the whole story.

**Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation**  
This review concludes that autonomous motivation—doing work because it is interesting or personally valued—is generally associated with better performance, persistence, and well-being than controlled motivation. It also argues that pay and management practices matter most when they support autonomy, competence, and relatedness rather than substitute for them.

**Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis**  
This meta-analysis found intrinsic motivation is a particularly strong predictor of performance quality, while extrinsic incentives are more predictive of performance quantity. That pattern supports giving real weight to interesting work, but not ignoring compensation or role structure.

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation**  
This meta-analysis found that expected, contingent tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, especially when rewards feel controlling. It is a direct caution against overweighting salary when the new work itself seems less engaging.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit**  
Better fit between a person and the job or organization is strongly associated with higher job satisfaction, stronger commitment, better performance, and lower intent to quit. If leading a small team is a long-standing goal, that leadership component may matter as much as topic-interest because it changes your overall fit.

**High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being**  
This study found higher income is more strongly related to how people evaluate their lives overall than to their moment-to-moment emotional well-being. For a modest pay increase, it suggests salary may improve the story you tell about your life more than the daily experience of doing the work.

**Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year**  
This later study challenged a simple income plateau and found experienced well-being continues to rise with income on average. Even so, it does not show that marginal salary gains dominate intrinsic job quality, autonomy, or fit in career decisions.

Meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, work engagement, organizational commitment, and overall well-being. The effect sizes are large enough to support the view that many people undervalue how much work content drives quality of life.

- **Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis**  
Intrinsic motivation is a robust predictor of performance, especially for quality, creativity, and persistence, while extrinsic incentives are more predictive of quantity. That pattern suggests that if the work itself is intellectually rich, intrinsic interest is especially valuable.

- **A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation**  
This meta-analysis found that some forms of external rewards, especially when experienced as controlling, can reduce intrinsic motivation. Higher pay is not inherently bad, but it is not a reliable substitute for engaging work.

- **Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work**  
People who experience their work as a calling report substantially higher meaning and satisfaction than those who see it mainly as a job. The paper is influential evidence that subjective orientation to work matters a lot, though it does not imply everyone should optimize only for meaning.

- **Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person-Job, Person-Organization, Person-Group, and Person-Supervisor Fit**  
Perceived fit with the role and organization strongly predicts job satisfaction, commitment, and intentions to stay or quit. This is relevant because wanting to lead a team is itself a major fit variable, not a minor side preference.

- **High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being**  
This paper found that income strongly improves life evaluation, but daily emotional well-being shows much weaker gains once basic comfort is secured. For already highly paid workers, a modest salary increase is less likely to matter than work design and day-to-day experience.

- **The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work**  
Deeply meaningful work can make people more willing to accept sacrifices, lower pay, and even exploitation. This is an important caution against focusing almost exclusively on interestingness, because meaning can sometimes cause people to underweight other important job attributes.

- *Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory*  
  This classic paper found that jobs high in skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback produce higher internal motivation and job satisfaction. It is foundational evidence that “interesting” work features are major drivers of how good work feels, not a nice-to-have after salary.

- *The Validity of the Job Characteristics Model: A Review and Meta-Analysis*  
  This meta-analysis showed that enriched job characteristics are reliably associated with internal work motivation, satisfaction, and performance-related outcomes. It supports giving interesting, engaging work substantial weight, though it does not imply other factors are irrelevant.

- *Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature*  
  This paper found that work design variables such as autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and social support have robust links to satisfaction, engagement, and performance. The practical implication is that “interestingness” matters a lot, but it is best thought of together with autonomy and social context rather than in isolation.

- *Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being*  
  This paper argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are basic psychological needs, and work that supports them tends to improve motivation and well-being. It is a strong theoretical reason to prefer roles that feel inherently engaging and growth-promoting over roles that are merely better paid.

- *A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation*  
  This review found that some kinds of extrinsic rewards, especially controlling or expected tangible rewards, can reduce intrinsic motivation for interesting tasks. That does not mean money is bad, but it does mean higher compensation does not automatically offset the downside of less engaging work.

- *The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature*  
  This meta-analysis found that pay is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is modest rather than dominant. For your decision, that is evidence against overweighting a somewhat higher salary if the day-to-day work itself seems meaningfully less interesting.

- *Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit*  
  This paper showed that person–job fit and related forms of fit are strongly associated with job satisfaction, commitment, and intentions to stay. It suggests that whether the work matches your interests and strengths is a central predictor of whether you will actually like the job over time.

rows.answer:
**Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory**  
This paper found that jobs high in skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback produce higher intrinsic motivation and job satisfaction. It is one of the strongest classic arguments that “interesting” work is not a luxury perk but a central driver of how good a job feels.

**Job Demands, Job Decision Latitude, and Mental Strain: Implications for Job Redesign**  
Karasek showed that demanding work is much less harmful, and often more energizing, when people have high control over how they do it. That means interesting, challenging work tends to pay off most when it comes with autonomy rather than just pressure or status.

**Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being**  
This review concludes that intrinsic motivation and the satisfaction of autonomy, competence, and relatedness are consistently associated with better well-being and performance than reliance on extrinsic rewards alone. The evidence supports giving heavy weight to whether the work itself is engaging, though not literally “almost exclusively.”

**The “What” and “Why” of Goal Pursuits: Human Needs and the Self-Determination of Behavior**  
This paper argues that people do better psychologically when pursuing intrinsically motivated goals than when chasing money, image, or status for their own sake. It directly supports the idea that a more interesting role can be better for overall well-being even when it is not the top option on compensation or prestige.

**High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being**  
Kahneman and Deaton found that higher income strongly improves life evaluation, but day-to-day emotional well-being rises much less and appeared to level off after a moderate income threshold in their data. This suggests that extra salary matters, but it may not compensate for years spent doing less enjoyable daily work.

**Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year**  
Killingsworth’s experience-sampling study found that experienced well-being continues to increase with income beyond the threshold reported in earlier work. The result weakens any simplistic “money stops mattering” claim, but it still does not show that pay dominates intrinsic interest, meaning, or autonomy.

**Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work**  
This paper distinguishes between seeing work as a job, a career, or a calling, and shows that people who see their work as a calling report greater meaning and attachment. It is more descriptive than causal, but it strongly aligns with the view that inherently engaging work has unusually large subjective value.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person-Job, Person-Organization, Person-Group, and Person-Supervisor Fit**  
This meta-analysis found that fit with the job and organization is robustly related to job satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover intentions. A role can therefore look better on paper yet still be worse if the actual work content and environment fit you less well.

rows.answer:
*Work Design Characteristics and Outcomes: A Meta-Analysis and Theoretical Review*  
Across many studies, jobs with more variety, significance, autonomy, and feedback showed substantially better motivation, satisfaction, performance, and lower strain. This is strong evidence that “interesting work” is a major driver of good outcomes, but it also implies that autonomy and responsibility matter alongside interest.

*Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis*  
Intrinsic motivation was a strong predictor of performance, especially for quality, creativity, and persistence, while extrinsic incentives were more predictive of sheer quantity. This supports your friends’ claim that engaging work is often undervalued, but not that compensation can be ignored.

*A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation*  
Expected, tangible rewards often reduced intrinsic motivation for tasks people already found interesting, particularly when rewards felt controlling. The paper is one of the clearest reasons to be cautious about choosing higher pay when it comes with less engaging work.

*High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being*  
Higher income was linked to better overall life evaluation, but day-to-day emotional well-being showed much weaker gains beyond a moderate income level. For someone already well-paid, a somewhat higher salary may matter less to lived experience than the nature of the work itself.

*Optimal Experience in Work and Leisure*  
This paper found that people often experienced “flow” more at work than in leisure when work offered challenge, skill use, and clear goals. It suggests that work that is genuinely absorbing can contribute disproportionately to everyday well-being.

*Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person-Job, Person-Organization, Person-Group, and Person-Supervisor Fit*  
Fit with the job and organization strongly predicted job satisfaction, commitment, performance, and intention to stay. The implication is that “interesting” is not enough in the abstract; the role has to fit your motives, values, and strengths.

*Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work*  
People who related to their work as a calling reported higher satisfaction and attachment than those who saw it mainly as a job or a career ladder. This supports prioritizing meaning and interest, while also showing that subjective orientation toward the work matters.

*A Dark Side of the American Dream: Correlates of Financial Success as a Central Life Aspiration*  
Placing stronger priority on financial success over intrinsic aspirations was associated with lower well-being. The paper does not say money is unimportant, but it directly cautions against letting salary differences dominate major life choices.

rows.answer:
- *Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory*  
  This paper introduced the Job Characteristics Model and found that variety, autonomy, task significance, and feedback strongly increase internal motivation and job satisfaction. It is one of the clearest foundations for the idea that “interesting” work matters a lot.

- *Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature*  
  This meta-analysis found that autonomy, task significance, skill variety, social support, and contextual features all predict satisfaction, motivation, performance, and strain. The broad takeaway is that interesting work is very important, but not the only thing that matters.

- *The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature*  
  This meta-analysis found only a small positive relationship between pay and job satisfaction. It suggests salary matters, but usually much less than people expect when predicting how happy they will be at work.

- *A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation*  
  This review found that some expected, tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks. It supports the idea that choosing a less interesting job mainly for more money can carry a real motivational cost.

- *Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis*  
  This meta-analysis found that intrinsic motivation is especially important for performance quality, while extrinsic incentives are more useful for performance quantity. For research-heavy work, enjoying the work itself appears particularly valuable.

- *Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work*  
  This paper found that people who see their work as a calling report higher work and life satisfaction than those who see it mainly as a job or career. It supports the importance of meaning and interest, not just compensation or status.

- *Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation*  
  This paper argues that durable motivation and well-being at work come from satisfying autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Its implication is that “interestingness” matters a lot, but a role that better supports growth, ownership, or connection can also be a better long-run fit.

rows.answer:
**Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory**  
This classic paper found that skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback strongly predict internal motivation and job satisfaction. It is one of the foundational pieces of evidence that “interesting” work characteristics matter a lot for how a job feels day to day.

**Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being**  
This review argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central drivers of motivation and well-being, often more durable than external incentives alone. In practical terms, work that is inherently engaging and lets you grow tends to support better long-run experience than work chosen mainly for pay.

**Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis**  
This meta-analysis found that meaningful work is positively associated with job satisfaction, work engagement, organizational commitment, and well-being, and negatively associated with turnover intentions. The evidence supports your friends’ view that interesting/meaningful work is often undervalued in career decisions.

**On the Meaning of Work: A Theoretical Integration and Review**  
This review integrates evidence showing that meaning at work arises from the self, relationships, job design, and broader purpose. Its main implication is that “interestingness” is not the only way work becomes deeply satisfying, but meaning-related features are consistently important.

**High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being**  
This paper found that income strongly improves how people evaluate their lives overall, but daily emotional well-being showed a plateau around $75,000 in that U.S. sample. It is often cited as evidence that more money helps, but less than people expect for moment-to-moment experience.

**Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year**  
Using experience-sampling data, this paper found that experienced well-being continued to rise with income above the previously claimed plateau. The practical takeaway is that compensation does matter, so “interesting work” should not be treated as the only thing that matters.

**Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction**  
This paper showed that people overweight salient differences when predicting happiness and neglect the many ordinary factors that shape daily life. Applied to job choice, it suggests you should be cautious about over-indexing on the most vivid feature of each option, whether that is salary, prestige, or “interestingness.”

**The Significance of Task Significance: Job Performance Effects, Relational Mechanisms, and Boundary Conditions**  
This research found that seeing one’s work as benefiting other people can increase persistence and performance. That matters here because leading a team can create a strong sense of impact even if the technical content is less intrinsically interesting.

rows.answer:
- **The Relative Importance of Intrinsic and Extrinsic Rewards as Determinants of Work Satisfaction**  
  This paper finds that intrinsic rewards—especially interesting work, autonomy, and opportunities to use one’s abilities—are stronger predictors of work satisfaction than extrinsic rewards like pay. It supports your friends’ core point that “interestingness” is often undervalued, though it does not imply other factors are irrelevant.

- **Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature**  
  This meta-analysis shows that job characteristics such as autonomy, task variety, task significance, and feedback are strongly related to job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, and performance. Its broader conclusion is that the nature of the work itself matters a great deal, but social and contextual features matter too.

- **Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis**  
  This meta-analysis finds that meaningful work is robustly associated with job satisfaction, work engagement, organizational commitment, and overall well-being, and negatively associated with turnover intentions. The evidence is strong that doing work you find meaningful or interesting has large benefits, but not that it should be pursued “almost exclusively.”

- **The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature**  
  This paper finds that pay is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is modest rather than dominant. In practical terms, a somewhat higher salary usually helps less than people expect, especially compared with major differences in the intrinsic quality of the work.

- **A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation**  
  This review shows that some kinds of extrinsic rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation, especially when they feel controlling rather than informational. It does not mean salary is bad, but it does suggest that chasing external rewards at the cost of interesting work can backfire psychologically.

- **Development of the Job Diagnostic Survey**  
  This foundational work shows that core job features—skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback—predict internal work motivation and satisfaction. It is one of the clearest empirical bases for the idea that the design and interestingness of the work itself are central to how good a job feels.

- **High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being**  
  This paper finds that higher income is more strongly linked to how people evaluate their lives than to their day-to-day emotional experience. That pattern is consistent with the idea that a higher-paying but less interesting job may look better on paper without making daily life feel better.

rows.answer:
**Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being**  
This paper argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are basic psychological needs, and environments that support them increase intrinsic motivation and well-being. In work terms, jobs that feel self-directed and growth-promoting are usually more satisfying than jobs chosen mainly for external rewards.

**Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory**  
This foundational study showed that jobs high in skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback produce higher internal motivation and job satisfaction. It is classic evidence that the nature of the work itself strongly shapes how good a job feels.

**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature**  
This meta-analysis found that work characteristics such as autonomy, task significance, social support, and feedback are strongly related to job satisfaction, motivation, and performance. The overall message is that intrinsic job design features are major drivers of work quality of life.

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation**  
This meta-analysis found that expected, controlling tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for interesting tasks. It supports the view that money and motivation from the work itself are not interchangeable.

**Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis**  
This meta-analysis found that intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance, especially for quality, creativity, and persistence, while extrinsic incentives are more useful for quantity. That suggests interesting work matters a lot if you care about sustained engagement and high-level output.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature**  
This meta-analysis found that pay is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is modest rather than large. It suggests higher salary helps, but usually not nearly enough to dominate all other job characteristics.

**Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work**  
This paper showed that people who experience their work as a calling report higher work satisfaction and stronger identification with what they do than those who see work mainly as a job or a career ladder. It is often cited as evidence that meaning and interest in the work itself can outweigh purely instrumental considerations.

rows.answer:
**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature**  
This meta-analysis found that autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and social support are strongly associated with job satisfaction, internal motivation, performance, and lower burnout/turnover intentions. It supports the claim that interesting, well-designed work matters a lot, but not that it should be valued almost exclusively.

**Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis**  
Across 40 years of studies, intrinsic motivation robustly predicted performance, especially quality and persistence, while extrinsic incentives were more predictive of quantity. This suggests interesting work is a major driver of doing good work, but not a universal substitute for all other job attributes.

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation**  
This meta-analysis found that expected, tangible external rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for tasks people already find interesting. It supports being cautious about trading away interesting work for money alone, though it does not show compensation is unimportant overall.

**Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis**  
Meaningful work was strongly linked to job satisfaction, work engagement, organizational commitment, and overall well-being, and negatively linked to turnover intentions. This is some of the clearest evidence that finding work interesting or meaningful has broad benefits for both day-to-day experience and longer-run outcomes.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature**  
This meta-analysis found a positive but modest relationship between pay and job satisfaction, much smaller than many people intuitively expect. It is evidence against treating a somewhat higher salary as decisive when the tradeoff is a substantially less interesting role.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person-Job, Person-Organization, Person-Group, and Person-Supervisor Fit**  
This meta-analysis showed that person-job and person-organization fit predict satisfaction, commitment, performance, and intention to quit. That means your desire to lead a small team is not a side issue: if leadership fit matters to you, it can materially affect which role is better.

**Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation**  
This review argues that autonomous motivation—doing work because it is interesting or personally valued—consistently predicts better performance, learning, persistence, and well-being than controlled motivation. It supports prioritizing interest and meaning, but frames pay, structure, and management as important insofar as they support or frustrate autonomy, competence, and relatedness.

rows.answer:
- **Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature**  
  This meta-analysis found that autonomy, task significance, feedback, skill variety, and related job-design features have substantial associations with job satisfaction, internal motivation, and performance. It is strong evidence that intrinsically engaging work characteristics matter a great deal for how good a job feels.

- **Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis**  
  This meta-analysis found that meaningful work is strongly related to job satisfaction, work engagement, organizational commitment, and life satisfaction, and negatively related to turnover intentions. It supports the view that meaning and interest at work are major predictors of well-being, though most of the evidence is correlational rather than fully causal.

- **Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work**  
  This paper shows that people who experience their work as a “calling” report substantially higher work and life satisfaction than those who see it mainly as a job or career. The results suggest that the felt significance and intrinsic appeal of work can matter independently of income or prestige.

- **A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation**  
  This meta-analysis found that expected tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for tasks that are already interesting, while verbal recognition can sometimes enhance it. It is one of the clearest findings supporting the idea that money can crowd out motivation when the work itself is engaging.

- **Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?**  
  This study found that scientists accept lower wages in exchange for nonpecuniary benefits such as freedom to publish and the chance to work on scientifically interesting problems. It is direct labor-market evidence that highly skilled people often place large monetary value on interesting work.

- **The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature**  
  This meta-analysis found that pay and job satisfaction are positively related, but only modestly. The implication is that compensation matters, but it is usually not the dominant factor in whether people like their jobs.

- **High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being**  
  This paper found that higher income improves how people evaluate their lives, but day-to-day emotional well-being showed little improvement beyond a moderate income level. For a “bit higher salary,” it suggests the well-being gain may be smaller than people often expect.

- **Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year**  
  This later study found that experienced well-being continues to rise with income over a wide range, challenging a strict income plateau. It implies that salary is not trivial, but it does not overturn the broader evidence that intrinsically rewarding work is very important.

- **Promotions and the Peter Principle**  
  This paper shows that firms often promote strong individual contributors into management even when their prior performance predicts worse managerial outcomes. It is relevant because leading a team is a different job, so the value of that offer depends heavily on whether you want the daily work of management itself.

rows.answer:
- *Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory*  
  This foundational paper found that skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback are strongly linked to internal motivation and job satisfaction because they make work feel meaningful and self-directed. It is one of the classic pieces of evidence that the substance of the work itself can matter more than external rewards for everyday motivation.

- *Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature*  
  Across many studies, job features tied to interesting work—especially autonomy, variety, task significance, and feedback—showed strong relationships with satisfaction, motivation, engagement, performance, and lower burnout. The paper also shows that interestingness is not everything, because social support, interdependence, and contextual conditions matter too.

- *A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation*  
  This meta-analysis found that expected, contingent tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for tasks people already find interesting. In practical terms, a somewhat higher salary may be less valuable than it appears when the work itself is highly engaging.

- *The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature*  
  This meta-analysis found that pay is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is modest rather than large. That suggests that once compensation is already good, incremental pay increases often do less for day-to-day satisfaction than people expect.

- *High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being*  
  This paper found that higher income is more strongly related to how people evaluate their life overall than to how they feel from moment to moment. That supports taking differences in lived work experience seriously, not just differences in salary.

- *Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis*  
  This meta-analysis found that intrinsic motivation is especially important for performance quality and persistence, while extrinsic incentives matter more for quantity-focused output. For research work, where creativity and depth matter a lot, interesting work may therefore be unusually valuable.

- *Promotions and the Peter Principle*  
  Using large-scale sales data, this paper found that firms often promote strong individual contributors into management even when prior individual performance does not predict managerial effectiveness and can even predict worse management outcomes. If leading a team is part of the appeal, this suggests treating “becoming a manager” as a separate career choice rather than assuming it is automatically the better next step.

rows.answer:
- **Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature.**  
Across many studies, autonomy, task variety, task significance, and feedback were strongly associated with job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, performance, and lower burnout/strain. This is strong evidence that how interesting and well-designed the work is matters a lot.

- **Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory.**  
This foundational paper showed that jobs high in skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback produce higher internal motivation and satisfaction. In plain terms, “interesting work” is not fluff; it is a core driver of how people feel and perform.

- **Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being.**  
This paper argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are basic psychological needs, and work that supports them improves motivation and well-being. It strongly supports prioritizing roles that feel inherently engaging and self-directed rather than optimizing for external rewards alone.

- **A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation.**  
This meta-analysis found that some kinds of external rewards, especially controlling tangible rewards, can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks. Higher pay is valuable, but it does not reliably substitute for interest and can sometimes crowd out enjoyment.

- **Goal Striving, Need Satisfaction, and Longitudinal Well-Being: The Self-Concordance Model.**  
People made more progress and experienced greater well-being when pursuing goals aligned with their genuine interests and values rather than externally pressured goals. That suggests choices that fit what you actually care about tend to work out better over time than choices driven mainly by salary or prestige.

- **Breaking Monotony with Meaning: Motivation in Crowdsourcing Markets.**  
Even a small increase in perceived meaning improved effort and output on otherwise dull tasks without changing pay. This is direct evidence that meaningfulness/interest can have real motivational power independent of compensation.

- **The Job Satisfaction–Job Performance Relationship: A Qualitative and Quantitative Review.**  
This review found a positive, though moderate, relationship between job satisfaction and job performance. Enjoying the work is not just a lifestyle preference; it is also linked to doing better at the job.

- **High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being.**  
This paper found that income was strongly related to life evaluation, while day-to-day emotional well-being showed much weaker gains beyond a moderate income level. It implies that extra salary matters, but not necessarily as much for everyday lived experience as the nature of the work itself.

- **Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year.**  
This later paper challenged the earlier “plateau” result and found continued gains in experienced well-being with higher income. The best reading of the income literature is not that money stops mattering, but that it is one important input among others rather than an automatic trump card.

- **Calling and Vocation at Work: Definitions and Prospects for Research and Practice.**  
This review summarizes evidence that experiencing work as a calling is associated with higher meaning, satisfaction, and commitment. It also cautions that people may over-tolerate downsides for meaningful work, so “interesting” is powerful but not something to optimize literally to the exclusion of everything else.

rows.answer:
- **The Validity of the Job Characteristics Model: A Review and Meta-Analysis**  
  This meta-analysis found that core job features such as skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback are consistently associated with internal work motivation and job satisfaction. It supports the view that inherently engaging work matters a great deal for how good a job feels, not just for performance.

- **Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature**  
  This paper showed that autonomy, task variety, task significance, and cognitively rich work are strongly related to satisfaction, motivation, and performance. It suggests that “interesting work” is not a vague luxury but a cluster of measurable job characteristics with sizable links to well-being.

- **The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature**  
  This meta-analysis found that pay is positively related to job satisfaction, but only modestly so. That is strong evidence against the idea that salary should dominate the decision once compensation is already good.

- **A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation**  
  Across experiments, expected tangible rewards tended to reduce intrinsic motivation for activities that people already found interesting. The paper supports the claim that external rewards do not fully substitute for genuinely engaging work and can sometimes crowd it out.

- **Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person-Job, Person-Organization, Person-Group, and Person-Supervisor Fit**  
  This paper found that person-job fit and person-organization fit are strongly associated with job satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover intentions. It is relevant because wanting to lead a team is part of what “fit” can mean, so role alignment may matter independently of both salary and topical interest.

rows.answer:
- *Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature*  
  This meta-analysis found that task variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback are strongly associated with job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, and lower turnover intentions. It supports the view that interesting, well-designed work matters a lot, but also shows that outcomes are best predicted by a bundle of job features rather than “interestingness” alone.

- *Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis*  
  Across decades of studies, intrinsic motivation reliably predicted performance, especially quality, creativity, and persistence, while extrinsic incentives were more tied to quantity. This suggests interesting work is often undervalued, but not that compensation and external rewards are irrelevant.

- *Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis*  
  Meaningful work was strongly linked to job satisfaction, work engagement, organizational commitment, and general well-being, and negatively linked to turnover intentions. The results are consistent with your friends’ claim that doing work you find interesting or meaningful is a major driver of a good career experience.

- *The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature*  
  This meta-analysis found that pay level has only a small positive relationship with job satisfaction. In practice, that means modest salary differences usually matter less for day-to-day work happiness than people expect.

- *Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person-Job, Person-Organization, Person-Group, and Person-Supervisor Fit*  
  Person-job and person-organization fit strongly predicted satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower intention to quit. That implies the best choice is not just about whether the work is interesting, but also whether the role fits your values and desired identity—such as becoming a team lead.

rows.answer:
- **Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis**  
  This meta-analysis found that meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, work engagement, organizational commitment, and life satisfaction, and negatively associated with turnover intentions. It supports the idea that interesting/meaningful work matters a lot for well-being, but it does not show that it should outweigh all other job features in every decision.

- **The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature**  
  This meta-analysis found that pay level has only a small positive relationship with job satisfaction. That suggests a modest salary increase usually matters less for day-to-day satisfaction than people expect, especially once compensation is already high.

- **Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature**  
  This paper found that autonomy, task significance, skill variety, feedback, and social support all predict satisfaction, motivation, and performance. Its main implication is that “interesting work” is important, but so are the surrounding design features of the job.

- **Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis**  
  This meta-analysis found that intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality, persistence, and creativity, while extrinsic incentives are more useful for quantity or simpler tasks. For research-heavy roles, it suggests genuine interest in the work is especially valuable.

- **A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation**  
  This paper found that some forms of external rewards, especially when they feel controlling, can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks. It does not mean money is bad, but it does warn that choosing primarily on marginal compensation can backfire if it comes with less engaging work.

- **Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation**  
  This review concludes that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central drivers of sustainable motivation and well-being at work. It argues against focusing on “interestingness” almost exclusively, because a role that is interesting but low in autonomy, growth, or human connection may still be a poor fit.

rows.answer:
- **Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory**  
  This paper found that jobs high in skill variety, task significance, task identity, autonomy, and feedback produce stronger internal motivation and job satisfaction. It supports the idea that interesting, meaningful work matters a lot, but not that it should be considered in isolation from autonomy and feedback.

- **Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature**  
  This meta-analysis showed that motivational job features such as autonomy, variety, significance, and feedback are strongly related to satisfaction, engagement, and performance. It also found that social features and work context matter independently, which argues against focusing almost exclusively on “interestingness.”

- **Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation**  
  This review argues that work is most motivating and healthy when it supports autonomy, competence, and relatedness, with intrinsic interest being only one part of that picture. A job can be fascinating yet still be a poor fit if it frustrates those other basic needs.

- **A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation**  
  This paper found that some kinds of external rewards, especially controlling tangible rewards, can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks. That supports caution about over-weighting compensation, but it does not imply pay is irrelevant or that all higher-paid roles are motivationally worse.

- **Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis**  
  This meta-analysis found that intrinsic motivation is especially important for performance quality, while extrinsic incentives are more predictive of performance quantity. The overall conclusion is that both matter, and the best outcomes often come from a good combination rather than a single dominant factor.

- **The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature**  
  This paper found that pay is positively related to job satisfaction, but only modestly. That means salary differences matter somewhat, yet usually much less than people assume when compared with work design and intrinsic features of the job.

- **On the Meaning of Work: A Theoretical Integration and Review**  
  This review argues that meaningful work comes from multiple sources, including the self, other people, the work context, and broader purpose. It suggests that leading a team can add meaning and identity even if the day-to-day technical work is less inherently interesting.

- **Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work**  
  This paper showed that people who experience their work as a calling report higher satisfaction and meaning than those who see it mainly as a job. But a calling can be expressed through leadership, impact, mentoring, or mission, not just through doing the most intellectually interesting tasks.

rows.answer:
- **Development of the Job Diagnostic Survey**  
  This paper found that jobs high in skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback produce higher internal motivation and job satisfaction. It supports the idea that “interesting” work matters a lot, but also shows that autonomy and feedback are separate major drivers rather than everything collapsing into interest alone.

- **Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being**  
  This review argues that intrinsic motivation is strongly linked to better well-being, persistence, and performance than extrinsic rewards alone. It does not support focusing on interest almost exclusively, because autonomy, competence, and relatedness are all treated as core psychological needs.

- **Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature**  
  This meta-analysis found that motivating work characteristics such as autonomy, task significance, and knowledge demands are strongly associated with satisfaction, motivation, performance, and lower strain. It also found that social and contextual features matter independently, which argues against a single-factor “interestingness dominates everything” view.

- **The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature**  
  This meta-analysis found that pay is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is modest rather than huge. That supports the claim that once compensation is already strong, differences in the work itself can matter more than additional salary.

- **High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being**  
  This paper found that higher income keeps improving how people evaluate their lives overall, but everyday emotional well-being shows much weaker gains after a certain level. For a high-paying career choice, that implies additional money may matter less to day-to-day experience than the nature of the work.

- **Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person-Job, Person-Organization, Person-Group, and Person-Supervisor Fit**  
  This meta-analysis found that fit predicts job satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover intentions across multiple kinds of fit. If “interesting” partly means a role matches your motives and strengths, the evidence says that fit is extremely important, but fit with manager and organization also matters.

- **Man’s Search for Meaning: The Case of Legos**  
  This experiment showed that even small differences in perceived meaning substantially changed how long people were willing to work and how much they produced, independent of pay. It is strong evidence that meaning/interest is often underrated, though it is a lab study rather than direct long-run career evidence.

- **Calling and Vocation at Work: Definitions and Prospects for Research and Practice**  
  This review summarizes evidence that experiencing work as a calling is associated with higher meaning and satisfaction. It also notes a downside: people with strong intrinsic attachment to work may tolerate lower pay or worse conditions, which is a warning against optimizing for interest alone.

rows.answer:
- *Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory*  
Jobs high in skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback produce higher internal motivation and job satisfaction. This is foundational evidence that “interesting” work design has large effects on how good work feels, independent of pay.

- *Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature*  
Across many studies, enriched jobs—especially those high in autonomy, variety, significance, and feedback—predict satisfaction, engagement, commitment, and performance. This supports prioritizing interesting work, but it also shows social and contextual features matter too, not just task interest alone.

- *Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis*  
Meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, work engagement, organizational commitment, and general well-being. The pattern suggests that meaning and interest are major levers in career decisions, though not the only ones.

- *A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation*  
External rewards can sometimes crowd out intrinsic motivation, especially when they feel controlling rather than informational. This implies that a higher salary does not automatically compensate for less interesting work and can even reduce enjoyment in some contexts.

- *The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature*  
Pay has a real but modest positive relationship with job satisfaction. The takeaway is that compensation matters, but usually much less than people intuitively assume when compared with richer job characteristics.

- *High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being*  
Higher income improves how people evaluate their lives, but moment-to-moment emotional well-being showed diminishing returns in this study. For your choice, that means salary may help you feel better about the decision on paper without necessarily making daily work more enjoyable.

- *Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year*  
Later evidence challenged the strict plateau story and found that experienced well-being keeps rising with income on average. This means money is not irrelevant, but it still does not overturn the strong evidence that day-to-day work content matters enormously.

- *Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work*  
People who see their work as a calling tend to report higher satisfaction and meaning than those who view it mainly as a job. This is consistent with the idea that deep interest or purpose can dominate day-to-day experience.

- *The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work*  
Very meaningful work can make people accept lower pay, worse conditions, or exploitation because they care so much about the mission. This is a caution against focusing on interest almost exclusively without also protecting compensation, status, and sustainability.

papers.paper: Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory
papers.description: Jobs high in skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback produce higher internal motivation and job satisfaction. This is foundational evidence that "interesting" work design has large effects on how good work feels, independent of pay.

papers.paper: Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature
papers.description: Across many studies, enriched jobs—especially those high in autonomy, variety, significance, and feedback—predict satisfaction, engagement, commitment, and performance. This supports prioritizing interesting work, but it also shows social and contextual features matter too, not just task interest alone.

papers.paper: Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis
papers.description: Meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, work engagement, organizational commitment, and general well-being. The pattern suggests that meaning and interest are major levers in career decisions, though not the only ones.

papers.paper: A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation
papers.description: External rewards can sometimes crowd out intrinsic motivation, especially when they feel controlling rather than informational. This implies that a higher salary does not automatically compensate for less interesting work and can even reduce enjoyment in some contexts.

papers.paper: The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature
papers.description: Pay has a real but modest positive relationship with job satisfaction. The takeaway is that compensation matters, but usually much less than people intuitively assume when compared with richer job characteristics.

papers.paper: High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being
papers.description: Higher income improves how people evaluate their lives, but moment-to-moment emotional well-being showed diminishing returns in this study. For your choice, that means salary may help you feel better about the decision on paper without necessarily making daily work more enjoyable.

papers.paper: Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year
papers.description: Later evidence challenged the strict plateau story and found that experienced well-being keeps rising with income on average. This means money is not irrelevant, but it still does not overturn the strong evidence that day-to-day work content matters enormously.

papers.paper: Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work
papers.description: People who see their work as a calling tend to report higher satisfaction and meaning than those who view it mainly as a job. This is consistent with the idea that deep interest or purpose can dominate day-to-day experience.

papers.paper: The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work
papers.description: Very meaningful work can make people accept lower pay, worse conditions, or exploitation because they care so much about the mission. This is a caution against focusing on interest almost exclusively without also protecting compensation, status, and sustainability.
